lenses.guidance: vv 39-40's 'acknowledge and take to heart' uses Hebrew imperatives stacked for solemnity — yada (know) and shub (return to heart). The grammar is catechetical: command first, then ground in 'the LORD is God; there is no other.'

vv 25-31's foreseeing of exile and restoration introduces the prophetic narrative form into Moses's farewell. The author sees beyond his own horizon, framing exile as covenant-conditional and return as the LORD's compassion.

vv 25-31's exile-and-return forecast condenses the covenant arc: scatter, seek with all the heart (v 29), restoration. The redemptive plan in miniature, written before the events, naming what later prophets would expand.

vv 35, 39's 'the LORD is God; there is no other' becomes the canonical refrain — Isa 45:5-6 ('I am the LORD, and there is no other') and 1 Cor 8:6 ('one God, the Father') echo this confession across the whole Bible.
